chunk_summary: The current challenge: social media feels saturated. What worked 1-2 years ago (generic talking head videos, '3 tips' content) no longer performs. Viewer preferences have shifted toward stronger information, better packaging, B-roll, text overlays, and trending audio. Platform fatigue is real.
content: So, the biggest challenge right now I see is people say it's saturated. There's never been this many people posting every day on social. the amount of content, it's like a fire hose coming out. And then with AI, with things like viral reels, like there's just this massive volume of content that's coming up every day and you've never had this much competition. So, how do you stand out in that environment? That's where people are kind of honestly struggling. And also, I've just noticed that like what used to work a year or two ago isn't necessarily working today. So, for example, with talking head videos on social, it's like people will say that they're not getting any reach. They blame the algorithm. Well, honestly, it's just the viewers their preferences have changed, right? And so, we're seeing a move towards like stronger information presented better, packaged better, and then also like B-roll and text and things like this in trending audio. And so, there's all kinds of things that you can do to really level up. And if you're using the playbook from a couple years ago, you're probably struggling. So a lot of noise everybody's creating content AI soulless content shorten attention spans or like the quality of content which might not just be production value but holding interest correct stuff happening. So it might just be assembled on your phone but the tactics of B-roll text transitions maybe switch up your environment not just plain talking head and then even platform fatigued audience is overwhelmed by choices.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why is social media harder now than a few years ago?
What content formats stopped working in 2025?
How have viewer preferences changed for short-form video?
Is the algorithm to blame for low reach?

chunk_summary: Counter-opportunity 1 — Go from 'how to' to 'how I': general advice no longer performs. Specific, personal stories that only you can tell stand out. Use the formula: 'How I [achieved specific result] by [doing specific thing].' This signals authenticity and beats generic AI-produced content.
content: So then what are some of the counter opportunities that's working right now to stand out? So I have a few that are just crushing right now ... one of the things that I think is always in style, it's been on style on YouTube forever is like talking head videos with good information. But the bar for information is higher now. So it's not just like three tips to do X. Like that doesn't perform as well on social anymore. But maybe you have a specific story like here's how my client here's how I did this in this time frame. And so anytime you can go from general to specific, it's going to perform better because then the audience feels this is real. It's not just some AI produced video or generic like we're seeing people that are using their avatar to create their talking head videos and it's super generic and so like what are the stories only you could tell like your experiences are actually what are going to make this stand out. So that would be the first thing. Let me let's give this formula to the community and that is go from how to to how I correct and finish the sentence of how I because as soon as you say how I made an extra $1,000 trying this strategy or how I lost 22 pounds in 45 days by doing X and what also is now you just got to tell your story and it's it also flips from kind of preaching which how to is fine how to get started but you're saying your methodology. So just so that listeners can swipe that like you use that as the headline and the opening line of one of your next pieces of content and see how it does.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why does 'how I' content perform better than 'how to'?
What makes content feel authentic vs AI-generated?
How can I turn my personal experience into a viral hook?
What's the difference between general and specific content?

chunk_summary: Counter-opportunity 2 — The '8 Mile Strategy': call out the viewer's specific pain point using their own language. Share your own vulnerability, mistakes, and failures as the hook. Example: 'how I lost $5,000 because of this stupid real estate mistake.' Leading with pain and failure builds instant connection and trust.
content: The other one that works really well right now for talking head is going to be meeting the viewer where they're at. So calling out the problem and if you've struggled with something and overcame it, those are great hooks. So for example, somebody might have done a few years ago, you know, three ways to buy a home in today's market. Maybe your video would be like, you know what, if you're feeling overwhelmed with today's real estate market, man, I was right there with you. I didn't know where to go or where to turn until I figured out this until I found this tool. Until I found this system and then now it's like, man, he was just like me. He struggled. I think a lot of people don't like to be vulnerable or give their struggle, but that's honestly where I've seen the biggest growth is where, hey, I struggled. I figured this out. I put together this system and now I'm here. People want to follow that because it's real. ... there's somebody even I was teaching this one time and they're like yo that's like the eight mile strategy. you know, call out the problem that you know is going to be on the viewer's mind. ... you can ask GPT for this like what are the pain points right now? What's your target audience? Who are you speaking to? But then what are their pain points? What's going on in their head? ... Pat Flynn actually told me this recently. He's like, if you can even use their language in that opening hook, it's going to bring them even more. He sends out an email to his entire database and asks them what they're struggling with. Now, not everyone's going to reply, but when they reply, he uses that exact verbiage in the videos. ... And when eight mile strategy, if you basically share your own vulnerability, you might look back and say, 'Okay, what was a time when you made a really stupid mistake? how I lost $5,000 because of the stupid real estate thing I did or how I lost the whole house or how I botched the contract or how I hurt my relationship, how I almost got divorced or how I almost whatever' because of calling out a mistake you made. And you'd also be calling your own story to the pain point. The person's like, 'That's where I'm at right now.' how I almost killed my business, how I lost my best employee...
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the 8 Mile strategy for content hooks?
How can sharing my failures help build a personal brand?
How do I find the exact pain points of my audience?
Why does vulnerability perform better than polished content?

chunk_summary: Counter-opportunity 3 — Move the strongest statement up front: when repurposing long-form clips, don't start with context. Take the boldest, most controversial, or punchiest line and put it in the first 3 seconds, even if it came later in the original conversation. This small tweak (a few minutes of editing) can 4x or 10x views.
content: And then I think like another strategy people use is chopping up long form. Like for example, this is a podcast. We could chop up clips posted on social. Those clips don't necessarily perform like they used to. And the biggest problem I see, Sean, is people have a strong statement or the boldest part of the piece of content in the middle of the piece. Yes. So we're setting up the context. We got maybe even a traditional hook. Then we get to the punchline. Nobody's there because they left in the first few seconds. So what I found great success in and I'm seeing this blow up everywhere is the best creators are taking the strongest statement putting it up front. Even if it didn't come that way in our conversation. Yep. So when you said 8 mile strategy is crushing. We would move that up front then give the context not the other way around. ... When people are cutting clips and like Opus clip which is a AI tool, right? It's going to go linear. So it allows you to rearrange it but most don't. So they just are starting wherever Opus clip tells you to start or trying to find the best place as opposed to moving it or repeating it too, right? So you could repeat that. And so I'll give you like an example. I was just teaching a workshop and I said man a lot of people say YouTube is saturated, podcast saturated, but what I found is nothing is saturated because most people suck at what they do. And so what I found is like yeah it's saturated for 95% of people who are not doing this. And then I went into my whole model teaching but like that line that's a bar. Nothing is saturated because most people suck at what they do. So, let me punch it up. Nothing is saturated because most people suck. Yeah, there you go. And cut the last part. Do that again. So, we posted the first time where I said the punch line a few lines in. Did Okay. Move the punch line up front. It crushed 4x the views right away. Same video. Now, I could take what you just said, cut that end off, make it even harder hitting. Yeah. It might do 10x the views. ... So I think the thing that's really cool about content today is small tweaks equal big leaps. This is a hook right now, right? But like that that is the best part because you can make one change that maybe takes you a couple minutes, bro. And all of a sudden you are reaching thousands of more people, maybe tens of thousands, maybe millions of people.
questions_on_this_chunk: How should I edit podcast clips for social media?
Why should I move the strongest statement to the beginning?
What's the biggest mistake when repurposing long-form content?
How can a small edit dramatically increase views?

chunk_summary: Neil's origin story: he started posting cringe content in 2018-2019 in the mortgage industry (a stale, boring industry). Even bad content worked because the bar was so low — 80 views that bring a high-value client is 'viral.' He got better through reps, found his voice, built confidence, and now runs a 7-figure business. Key lesson: start before you're ready; motivation comes after you see results.
content: So I hit record 2018 2019 in there and it was cringe. Like you get the Facebook memories, you know, on your phone, you're like you can't even watch the video. How is anyone else going to watch it? you know, but and those even worked at the time, but I didn't know how cringe it was, you know, and I think that's the thing, you don't get much reach anyways because it's a terrible video. But most people are just scared to do the thing, so they don't put it out there. And I just said, you know what? I'm going to try. I'm going to go for it anyway. And it's because I noticed the trend in my industry was shifting. ... I was watching guys like Gary Vee saying, 'Hey, this is a big massive opportunity for people in stale industries.' So, anytime you could do something new or innovative in a stale business, you're going to crush. ... I was thinking mortgage real estate, boring. Nobody's really doing content. Let me be the nerd, the information guy who brings the tips and the tricks to the people. Problem was, bro, is I couldn't communicate properly on camera. Didn't have the confidence. And so at a certain point, it just becomes about reps. So, anyways, I just started doing it, cringe at first and just got better over time and then along the way figured out my voice, got the confidence down...
questions_on_this_chunk: How did Neil Dhingra get started in social media?
What should I do if my first videos are embarrassing?
Why are stale industries a big opportunity for content creators?
How many views count as 'viral' for a high-ticket business?

chunk_summary: The 'only 9%' stat: only 9% of real estate agents post listing videos. Most industries are far from saturated — the majority are sitting on the sidelines. For people in 'stale' industries (mortgage, real estate, window washing), just showing up with content is an unfair advantage. The bar is still incredibly low.
content: You know what's interesting is I think where on the one hand, people think social media is saturated. On the other hand, I think for stale industries, it's just getting started. National Association of Realtors said that only 9% of agents are doing listing videos. Only 9%. That's wild. So, you would think as we are going into 2026 that oh man, everybody in real estate has got to be creating content, posting content, putting it out there. No, only one in 10. And of course, you see some creative, funny, you know, listing video in your Instagram feed and you compare yourself to them and it, you know, kind of goes viral. But like so many people are just sitting on the sidelines and they need to get in the game. And what I love that you said was you said you posted your first videos and they were cringe, but they even worked. What do you mean? So even bad content will perform because the bar is so low. And so you think about quality not quantity in terms of the quality of the viewer. So I'm in a high margin business. Each client has a big impact on my bottom line. I'm not selling t-shirts. So for example, if I get 80 views on a video, but one of the people in there is like, 'Hey man, loved your video on this. Would love to have a conversation.' That's viral for me. Yes. You know, 80 is viral if you get a client from it. And the client is worth thousands of dollars.
questions_on_this_chunk: What percentage of real estate agents post video content?
Is social media really saturated for all industries?
How can bad content still generate business?
How many views do I need to get a client?

chunk_summary: Building a personal brand through the 5 Fs: Food, Faith, Family, Fitness, Fashion. Use an 80/20 or 70/30 split — 70-80% business/niche content, 20-30% personal connection content. Weave personal interests (sports teams, hobbies, food preferences) into business content. Contrast makes you memorable. People connect with you over shared interests, not just your tips.
content: So you were talking about personal brand. You said it was a little bit tough especially when I started sharing personal information. What is your opinion of we're getting online we're planting our flag in a niche or an expertise especially as business owners or service providers. ... what percentage are you encouraging people to weave in different things? And I think you have like five Fs. What are the things you're weaving in to build a personal brand? I would go 8020 if 7030 if possible. So, there's got to be contrast. So for example, the more contrast the better with the personal brand. So here's a guy who does mortgage or like stale. I'm also Indian. Cool. That's one piece of contrast. I also don't dress like the people in my industry. That's another piece of contrast. I also like music and share some of those things. ... So all these little things give it this whole contrast feel. It's like think about some of the biggest brands. Hormozi this guy is a genius but he also looks like just a gym bro. He's ripped. He's in a wife beater every day. That's huge contrast. So those are the brands that are going to stand out. ... So for personal content, I would share food. These are the five Fs. Food, faith, family, fitness, fashion. Okay. Now, you could swap one of the Fs out for whatever your thing is. ... There's a guy in our group, he's into hiking in Denver. He does the tips on the hike. The algorithm is so good that it's going to show it to people who are outdoorsy. There's people who are the biggest Dallas Cowboys fans. Like, dude, just wearing the hat or the jersey in some of your content, someone's going to give you a little bit more attention because you have an affinity with you, like you share the same interests. So, I just would think about not only doing actually intentional personal content, but just weaving in some of your interest into the business content.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the 5 Fs of personal branding?
How much personal content should I mix with business content?
Why does contrast make a personal brand stand out?
How can I weave personal interests into professional content?

chunk_summary: The deeper connection: people rarely approach creators about business tips; they approach about shared personal interests. Gary Vaynerchuk's brand strength comes from shared personal details (wanting to buy the Jets, garage sale finds, family). Followers aren't equal — deep connection drives opt-ins and conversions far better than vanity metrics.
content: And what's crazy, bro, is there's people who will connect with you and they'll come up to you and you probably had this experience or me and I see it all the time where someone will tell me something personal. Yes. That they remember from social. They rarely come up to me and say, 'Hey, I loved your video on buying that rental property.' They're like, 'Here's some contraband.' You know, it happens all the time. Those touch points. Oh, you're also a fan of the Wu-Tang. Oh, you also go to a Banya, which is a European steam bath, or you're you also love Jesus. You also like that is it. They'll be like, 'Hey, your tips are good, but bro, like, you know, I know you've been on a fitness journey. How many people have connected with you because of your fitness?' ... Hermosi's most viewed YouTube short last time I checked was about Chipotle double protein. Yeah. Crazy. And he stopped doing those. He told me in a podcast he said hey we don't see as many optins from those. I would argue that it fits his overall strategy and he's not seeing the direct benefit, but indirectly people are more connected to him. They would give him more of their attention because of the Chipotle video. It's an interesting point you make. You're maybe not correlating that to optins, but the thing you could correlate it to is the brand and the connection. ... all followers are not equal. ... Gary has like this deep connection level of super fan. Correct. And there like I've never seen an ad perform better than a image of me and Gary for an opt-in. ... So what does that tell me? Like some people's brands are stronger than others. Much stronger. And because of the fact that we know things about Gary, I would argue the reason why Gary's brand is important is because we know he wants to buy the New York Jets. We know he loves freaking trash swap meets and stuff. We know like he talks about his family and this and that and he does all these things and we have a connection with him because of it. And if he didn't if he chose not to share those and just the business tips, maybe it wouldn't be there.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why do people remember personal content more than business tips?
How did Gary Vee build such a strong personal brand?
Are all followers equally valuable?
Why did Hormozi's Chipotle video perform so well?

chunk_summary: The Anti-AI strategy: AI is powerful for amplifying and organizing ideas, but not for replacing you. The content crushing right now is your unique take on the news — show a headline screenshot in the first 3 seconds, then give your personal point of view. You must have a point of view. If you don't stand for something, you stand for nothing. Polarizing (respectful) content creates conversation, which platforms reward with reach.
content: So, speaking about personal brand in depth, you're on this anti-AI strategy thing. Yeah. Because everyone's overhyping AI. Now, AI is powerful. Can help you write some scripts. It can help you write some hooks. AI video editing is cool. AI avatars, whatever. But everyone's worried about AI making content feel soulless. true generic. But when you're talking about like anti-AI strategy, one of your biggest things, just going a little bit deeper on this, is the power of authenticity of human content and why vulnerability beats polish in 2026. Expand on this for listeners that might be looking at AI a little bit worried. Yeah. So, I think what you want to do is, you know, use AI to amplify the message, clean it up, organize your thoughts. That's how I do it. But I'm not using it to replace me. You know what I mean? though in the content for example one piece of content that's crushing social right now is if you can give your take on the news so for example I've had some of my top performing videos on short form where I will take a headline from DC or something that's affecting my people my audience the industry and I will put that screenshot of the article and the tip for this the super tactical tip is to put the image of the headline in the first three seconds of the video and say guys did you see this did you hear about this and then I will repeat thing, but then I'm adding in my unique POV, point of view. You need to have a point of view. Like, you can't just be the news guy. ... I don't have to be first. There could be a bunch of news that's hard-hitting, breaking news vibe, but then I can just give my point of view. ... I think people are scared to give their opinion because they're going to piss somebody off. So, you if you want to stay away from politics, totally fine. I understand that. But I found if you don't stand for something, you stand for nothing. And it just gets lost in this void of soulless content. ... I would rather lose a few followers and give my opinion, not being rude, but give my opinions on X than just to sit quiet.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the anti-AI content strategy?
How can I use AI without making content soulless?
How do I give my take on the news in content?
Should I share my opinions even if they're polarizing?

chunk_summary: Why polarizing content spreads: Meta platforms have stated that conversation drives distribution. If content is vanilla and nobody comments, it dies. But if it sparks debate (respectfully), it gets shared and spreads. The key is how you say it — respect and nuance matter. Disagreeing with your own 'side' is uniquely attention-grabbing.
content: You know what's funny is some of these things I think are going to be the only way to break through going forward is a willingness to jump right in the middle of what you're saying, right in the middle of issues. A willingness knowing that there's going to be polarizing feelings on both sides and especially meta platforms Instagram and Facebook have already said that conversation is why posts will spread. Yeah. and if things are being shared and so if something is vanilla and nobody has anything to say then it just will die but if somebody goes polarizing each way and they're debating I think it's maybe removing yourself in a way you're almost like a fire starter you're kind of you could still present the information where you present both sides even you know some people are saying this some people are saying this and people will start fighting in your comments not that you're trying to pick a fight but that debate is stepping into something that actually matters ... So I've had things where the honestly the people who I respect the most when it comes to political things are people who will disagree with their own political party. Right. So you can't just like otherwise you're just like wearing the same you're looking you're saying the same thing everybody else is saying. And so if you have a unique point of view and if you are a Republican or a liberal it doesn't matter and you would say something that would go against your own party this is very unique. This will stand out. you'll get a debate going. And what I found is people will say, 'Hey, I love the way you explained that.' I like your perspective on that. Hey, Neil, I respect the fact that you said it when nobody else would say it. So, you'll lose a few people for sure who are kind of triggered, but you'll gain way more than you lose.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why does controversial content spread better?
How do platforms reward conversation and debate?
Should I disagree with my own industry or political side?
How do I handle losing followers for sharing opinions?

chunk_summary: The 3C System overview: 1) Content — high-quality, specific, opinionated content. 2) Connection — personal content that builds relatability (5 Fs, behind-the-scenes, stories). This is the C most people miss. 3) Conversion — don't sell the final offer directly. Add a middle step (webinar, lead magnet, free event) and sell that as if it's the only thing.
content: I want to talk about the 3C system reveal. So, you do have a three-C system that took you from cringe to seven figures. Can you walk us through these three C's? What's number one? Number one is content. And so, you want to do better content, content that would actually get you results. And so, what are the things that are working right now? What are the trends that are working right now? Your unique point of view, all the things we're talking about. That's going to be the content pillar. The next C is going to be connection. And this is the one that I think people miss. And honestly, I think people only do one. Like they might be doing all conversion content, but they're not doing any connection content, you know? So, you're not going to get the results if you don't do all three. So, the second one is connection, and that would be the personal things you share. How you connecting with the audience, maybe some behind the scenes. I noticed you share a lot about your family, your kids, what you guys are doing on the weekend, what you're doing in your time off. That could be all behind the scenes stories, content, and then you could also choose to make some intentional videos about connection. And one of my best performing videos from a personal standpoint was when I got my daughter tickets to Taylor Swift, how much I spent on it, you know, like what how she was so excited. It made her year. She'd been dying to go for forever and I chose to highlight that. That was really cool. Moment father, daughter event, Taylor Swift. How many points of connection? So many different things.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the 3C system for social media success?
Why is connection content the missing piece for most creators?
What counts as connection content?
How did a Taylor Swift video help Neil's brand?

chunk_summary: The stories-to-reels shift: content that used to only be posted in Stories now needs to go in the main feed as Reels. Story views are declining across the board, while Reels offer the chance to reach new people and have a longer shelf life. Don't save your personality content for Stories — post it publicly.
content: I would say the big shift, this is a big content shift for 2025 and beyond is many things that you would only put in stories now need to be posted in the feed. I every real every content creator is telling me that their story views are low. Yeah. They're down. They're having they're not reaching as many people as they used to because there's a lot of competition in stories and the algorithm is tough. But on reels, that same video that you thought was only good for your small audience on stories, it might blow up. You'll reach more of your followers, plus you have the chance to reach new people, which you cannot do in stories. And then of course, longer shelf life, it sometimes it could keep going and then if it takes off, it really could keep going for weeks.
questions_on_this_chunk: Should I still post on Instagram Stories?
Why are Story views declining?
Should I move my Stories content to Reels?
What's the shelf life difference between Stories and Reels?

chunk_summary: The Conversion C — The Dating Method: don't sell the final offer directly ('let's get married'). Add a middle step — a free resource, webinar, lead magnet, or live event — and sell that as the only thing. Nobody wants to book a call with a stranger. Create micro-commitments before the big ask. Focus on giving value, not extracting it. Neil's 7-figure business was built on selling the middle step, not the final offer.
content: And then we'll get into conversion. Okay. The last C. And so here's the big thing on conversion. Most people are going straight for the sale. They're straight for the jugular. Like call me. And so what I found, Sean, is people are selling the thing on social that the audience doesn't want. So in my business, they're selling an appointment, a call. Unfortunately, nobody wants to talk to you yet. They would rather do something else. So I think about this. What is the thing you want eventually? You want a sale, you want to be paid. What would come before that? A conversation. What would come before that? And maybe it's a webinar, maybe it's a lead magnet, something like that. And so the biggest shift I've ever made in business, this made me all the money, and it was selling that thing. What I realized is the best marketers, the people that make the most money on social are not looking for sales. They're looking for a signal. So you need to move back up the truck to like one or two or three steps before the sale and sell that thing as if that's the only thing. So guys, I have this free resource. I spent some time putting it together. If you want to grab it, just comment the word home and I'll send it to you. There's nothing in there about calling me, working with me. I never even mentioned myself. It's all about the free resource ... So if you want to succeed on social media in 2026, you need to use what's called the dating method. And that's what you're talking about here. Can we have a conversation? How about a coffee before you try and close, bro? Like, you know what I mean? Like, I met my wife at a company party at the Bellagio was a mortgage company. She was working here. I was in Reno. We got to meet there and I'm like, what if I just went up to her Sean? I was like, 'Yo, you want to go upstairs?' I don't know. Slap you like, 'Yeah, maybe you would pull it off. I would not like.' And so I wouldn't have a family if that was the case. What where is the romance in our business? It's all about closing. ... You can't spend your days talking to people who are 6 months away from buying. But you can use scalable content and all these things that you're doing to speak to those people and keep them in your funnel and build trust through multiple conversations. First, social media and YouTube is multiple conversations. Create micro commitments before the big ask. Focus on giving value, not extracting it.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the dating method for social media sales?
Why shouldn't I ask for a call directly in my content?
What should I offer instead of a sales appointment?
How do I build a middle step in my sales funnel?

chunk_summary: Final lessons and myths: going viral doesn't necessarily change your business — it depends on the goal and CTA of that specific video. Don't dabble in content; dominate with intention. The market is splitting into feast or famine: the top 1% crush it, everyone else gets smoked out. You can win part-time with intentionality. Start sharing personal connection content earlier. Always have an easy next step (lead magnet) available 24/7.
content: What's one thing you do differently if you were starting over today? So, one thing I would do different is I would put in more in the beginning I was really hesitant to share all these things on the personality and the connection pieces. I would start doing that earlier on and incorporating that into my business content. The other thing I would do is I didn't do this initially, but I realized that I did not have a next step for many years. Like I was just making the content and the only step was to book an appointment with me. And so I was missing that easy next step. What's the thing that people could download or opt in for at 1 in the morning in their underwear at home? Yes. And get it right then. And get it on demand and make it easy. I was making it too hard to work with me. What's the biggest myth about going viral you want to bust? So, the biggest thing is like people think it's going to change your business. And I've had some of the most viral content do not much for my business. It all depends on like what was the purpose, what was the call to action in the video. I had a video go viral where I talked about assumable mortgages. I don't even make money on assumable mortgages. So, it did honestly nothing for me, but maybe brought me a few more followers. So, you have to think about like what's your goal of the content? And I think a lot of people want more followers. that doesn't necessarily mean more business. What's one prediction for where personal branding is heading? I think it's heading into basically the top 1% being able to really crush it and then everybody else getting smoked out. So it's like a feast or famine thing. People are saying it doesn't work anymore. Then somebody else is saying it's never worked better. It's like that's where it's changing right now. It's not for everybody. It's for the people who want to be intentional, which is why you're watching this episode. It's good. If you're going to dabble, you're going to lose. But if you make the mental switch that I'm going to dominate, I'm going to stop dabbling. I'm going to start dominating and I'm going to put in the energy and take it seriously, you can win.
questions_on_this_chunk: Does going viral actually grow your business?
What would Neil do differently if starting over?
Why is an easy next step so important in content?
Is personal branding becoming harder or easier?
